lines.en_lit: a leper’s guilt-offering that was slaughtered not for its own sake we have arrived at the dispute between Rabbi Meir and Rabbi Elazar and Rabbi Shimon Rabbi Meir, who said he brings another and starts from the beginning, here too, he brings another and slaughters
And of Rabbi Elazar and Rabbi Shimon, who say he begins from the place where he stopped, here he has no remedy.
Rav Ḥisda strongly objects to this: But isn’t it written: it difficult

was taught in accordance with Rabbi Yoḥanan: a leper’s guilt-offering that slaughtered not for its own sake, or did not put some of its blood on thumbs, this is raised on the altar and requires libations And must another guilt-offering to make him eligible, And Rav Ḥisda said to you: What Must, he must but he has no remedy.

But would a tanna teach: Must, he has no remedy? Yes, and it was taught bald nazirite, Beit Shammai say: He must the passing of a razor and Beit Hillel say: He need not the passing of a razor. And Rabbi Avina said when Beit Shammai say: Must, he must he has no remedy.

disagrees with Rabbi Pedat. As Rabbi Pedat said: Beit Shammai and Rabbi Elazar said the same thing, Beit Shammai that which we said, Rabbi Elazar as it was taught has no thumb or big toe never ritual purity. Rabbi Elazar says: puts it on the spot and fulfills Rabbi Shimon says: If he put it on the left he has fulfilled
The Sages taught: shall take of the blood of the guilt-offering, might in a vessel; the verse states: “And put.” Just as putting with the priest’s own so too, taking with the priest’s own

might even on the altar, so the verse states: “For as the sin-offering is, so is the guilt-offering” Just as a sin-offering requires a vessel so too, a guilt-offering requires a vessel. And you find that you say: The leper’s guilt-offering two priests collect its blood; one by hand, and one in a vessel. The one who collected in a vessel comes to the altar and the one who collected by hand comes to the leper

We learned there: And all render ritually impure the garments And these is burned in the place of the ashes, the statement of Rabbi Elazar and Rabbi Shimon. And the Rabbis say: They do not render garments impure and they are not burned in the place of the ashes, except for the last since atonement is completed with it.
Rava inquired before Rav Naḥman: How many goats does he send away? He said to him: Does he send away his flock? said to him:
